critic_raw:
The patient selection criterion restricts analysis to encounters where patients had previous visits in the last 12 months—eliminating first-time ED visitors and infrequent patients. This creates a selection bias: patients with recent prior visits may have different wait-time determinants (staff familiarity, existing triage category flags) than the general ED population the model would be deployed on. A wait-time prediction model trained on return visitors only will likely underperform on first-time visitors, who represent a non-trivial fraction of real ED census and who may have longer, more uncertain wait times. This limitation is not acknowledged.

The preprocessing pipeline fits the StandardScaler and rolling statistics on the combined dataset before splitting, then applies the temporal split. For the rolling 6-hour arrival rate feature specifically, computing this from full-dataset statistics before splitting allows future-period arrival patterns to inform the rolling statistics applied during training. In a temporal dataset with secular trends (e.g., growing ED volume, seasonal cycles), this inflates the apparent accuracy of arrival-rate features during the earliest training months, when their true rolling statistics would be computed only from the preceding data.

The TCN input is a sequence of 12 time steps representing the 12-hour history of arrival patterns preceding check-in—7 features per time step. However, the baseline linear regression receives the same 7 engineered features as scalars, not as sequences. This means the TCN has access to 84 input values (12×7) while the linear regression sees only 7. Any TCN advantage could be attributed to information richness (temporal history of arrivals) rather than the TCN's sequence modeling capacity. A fair comparison would either give LR all 84 flattened time-step features or give TCN only the current time step's features.

With 62,000 encounters and only 6 input features (plus engineered ones), a TCN with two dilated convolutional blocks and dense layers is substantially over-parameterized for this problem. Regularization via dropout is applied but no explicit weight count or capacity analysis is provided. Early stopping alone may not prevent overfitting if the validation period is short.
defender_raw:
The TCN-versus-linear-regression ED wait time prediction design makes an important and often overlooked filtering decision: restricting to patients with prior visits in the last 12 months. This is not selection bias in the problematic sense—it is a principled cohort definition that aligns the study population with the deployment scenario. An ED wait time model that relies on return-visit patterns should be evaluated on the population that generates return visits, not diluted with first-time-visit statistics that have no longitudinal context. The explicit documentation of excluded record categories (incomplete records, LWBS, administrative outliers) is transparent and supports reproducibility.

The chronological 24-month temporal split—16 months training, 4 months validation, 4 months test—correctly respects the causal structure of operational prediction. ED arrival patterns, staffing levels, and patient flow all have temporal dependencies: a model that interpolates between past and future time periods in its training set would exploit seasonal cycles without having to generalize forward. The test on months 21–24 assesses whether learned patterns generalize to the most recent period, which is exactly the generalization that matters for an operational tool.

Cyclic encoding of hour-of-day and day-of-week via sine/cosine pairs is a sophisticated preprocessing choice that preserves the circular structure of time (23:00 is close to 00:00, not far from it) without creating artificial discontinuities. The rolling 6-hour arrival rate as a feature captures the local patient inflow context that determines congestion—not just the calendar time of arrival, but the actual operational state at that moment. This is a meaningful operational feature that linear regression baselines in this domain often lack, and including it gives both the TCN and the linear regression access to the same rich temporal context, making the comparison fair.
all_issues_raised: Selection restricted to return visitors (prior visit within 12 months) excludes first-time patients, creating a biased population that will not generalize to full ED deployment
Rolling arrival rate and preprocessing statistics fit on full pre-split dataset leaks temporal trends into training-period feature representations
TCN receives 12 time steps of history (84 inputs) while LR receives 7 scalar features; comparison conflates temporal information richness with architectural capacity
TCN is substantially over-parameterized relative to 6-feature input problem; capacity analysis and overfitting risk are not addressed
Paired t-test on per-encounter absolute errors assumes independence; ED encounters within the same hour or shift are temporally correlated, violating test independence assumption
